questions.question: Which Spring binding mechanism is closest to ASP.NET Core's IOptions<T> pattern?
choices: @ConfigurationProperties on a record or class that binds a configuration section as a typed object.
questions.explanation: @ConfigurationProperties binds a configuration section to a typed object (record or class) - the same role IOptions<T> plays in ASP.NET Core. @Value is closer to manually pulling individual values from IConfiguration.

questions.question: You set app.payments.url in application.yml, also export APP_PAYMENTS_URL=foo, and pass --app.payments.url=bar on the command line. Which value wins?
choices: Startup fails because of the conflict.
questions.explanation: Spring's precedence is CLI > env vars > profile files > base file. Command-line arguments win over both environment variables and configuration files - matching ASP.NET Core's typical precedence.

questions.question: What is the closest Spring concept to ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development?
choices: Activating the 'dev' profile via SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=dev or --spring.profiles.active=dev; Spring then layers application-dev.yml on top of application.yml.
questions.explanation: Spring profiles are the environment switch. Activating 'dev' layers application-dev.yml over application.yml - the same shape as ASP.NET Core's appsettings.Development.json layering. Note: Spring allows multiple active profiles simultaneously, unlike .NET's single-valued environment.
